citeinfo.title: Flow Through System (FTS) and Spectral Absorption / Attenuation Sensor (ACS) Data from Boat Mapping Transects within the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta

descript.abstract:
The goal of this study was to develop a suite of inter-related water quality monitoring approaches capable of modeling and estimating the spatial and temporal gradients of particulate and dissolved total mercury (THg) concentration and particulate and dissolved methyl mercury (MeHg) concentration in surface waters across the Sacramento / San Joaquin River Delta (SSJRD). This suite of monitoring approaches included: a) data collection at fixed continuous monitoring stations (CMS) outfitted with in-situ sensors, b) spatial mapping using boat-mounted, flow-through, sensors and c) satellite-based remote sensing. The focus of this specific Child Page is to present all data collected during the underway boat mapping component of the study.
The data compiled here represent measurements from a suite of field sensors collected as part of a flow-through system (FTS) integrated into a high-speed boat monitoring package. Data was collected during four mapping cruises representing seasonal conditions between October 2019 and May 2021. Sensor measurements were made at a 1-second frequency while underway at speeds of up to 35 mile per hour (mph) along three nautical routes crossing the SSJRD. Each mapping event was conducted over a three-day period centered on a LANDSAT8 satellite overpass (Landsat 8 | U.S. Geological Survey (usgs.gov)). All measurements were geo-referenced using a GPS running concurrently with the boat FTS and were merged in real time during data collection using a datalogger. The measurements collectively provide information about the amount and character of the substances in the water that relate to mercury species concentration and distribution throughout the Delta.
The package consisted of two flow-paths containing debubblers and flow controllers to ensure consistent and comparable measurements were obtained from multiple sensors deployed both in series and in parallel along the two flow-paths.
The first flow-path (referred to as the FTS flow-path) pulled river water through a 179-micron screen to protect the instruments from large particles. The flow-path included an EXO2 (YSI EXO2 Multiparameter Water Quality Sonde | ysi.com), a thermosalinigraph (SBE 21 SeaCAT Thermosalinograph | Sea-Bird Scientific - Overview | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)), fluorescent dissolved organic matter (fDOM) and chlorophyll fluorometers (ECO Fluorometer | Sea-Bird Scientific - Overview | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)), a submersible ultraviolet spectrophotometer (SUNA V2 Nitrate Sensor | Sea-Bird Scientific - Overview | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)), a transmissometer (C-Star Transmissometer | Sea-Bird Scientific - Overview | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)), and an algal classification sensor (FluoroProbe (Chlorophyll) - bbe moldaenke (10cells.com)). The sensors collectively provide information about the optical properties of the dissolved and particulate substances in the water and correspond to similar measurements conducted at the CMS locations used in this study.
The second flow-path (referred to as the ACS flow-path) pulled river water in the same manner as the FTS flow-path, but the water was further split into two paths, one that contained a 0.2 micron filter and the other that did not contain a filter. Each flow-path was equipped with a spectrometer (ac-s Spectral Absorption and Attenuation Sensor | Sea-Bird Scientific - Overview | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)) capable of measuring attenuation and absorption spectra throughout the visible wavelength range. This set-up allowed for the simultaneous measurement of absorption (a) and attenuation (c) within the filter-passing (dissolved) fraction and the particulate fraction of the water.
descript.purpose:
This data release is in partial fulfillment of an agreement between the U.S. Geological Survey and the University of California, Merced, related to a study funded through the California Department of Fish and Wildlife (PROPOSAL #: P1896056-01, TITLE: High Resolution Temporal and Spatial Mapping of Mercury in Surface Waters of the San Francisco Bay Delta), as part of the California Proposition 1 Water Quality, Supply, and Infrastructure Improvement Act of 2014. The overarching goal of this study was to develop models that can estimate surface-water concentrations of four mercury species (particulate and filter-passing fractions of total mercury and methylmercury) using a trio of high-resolution water-quality data collection platforms.
The purpose of the files herein is to provide the full spatial datasets for the four boat-based mapping events (three days each) and the underlying information for how these measurements were processed. These resulting data files can be used to generate high spatial resolution maps of all measurements and can be integrated into empirical model that estimate mercury species concentrations and to generate high spatial resolution mercury concentration maps from these boat-based measurements.
descript.supplinf: Any use of trade, firm, or product names is for descriptive purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the U.S. Government.

idinfo.accconst: None. Please see 'Distribution Info' for details.
idinfo.useconst: None. Users are advised to read the dataset's metadata thoroughly to understand appropriate use and data limitations.

citeinfo.title: Quantifying fluxes and characterizing compositional changes of dissolved organic matter in aquatic systems in situ using combined acoustic and optical measurements

Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton and related water-quality constituents in the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale: 2020 High-resolution mapping surveys

attracc.attraccr: Attribute accuracy for boat-based measurements is evaluated through comparisons with redundant measurements collected using different instruments and through relationships with established laboratory measurements. The data herein should be considered ‘processed’ but requiring further evaluation for interferences and signal to noise across the wavelength range.

dataqual.logic:
The data have been checked to ensure that they fall within expected ranges of reporting and suspect data have been removed from the dataset and are either reported as blank cells or as gaps in the reporting DateTime. The user should be aware that although great efforts were expended to eliminate measurements affected by bubbles and other interferences, isolated periods of questionable sensor measurements may persist within the files. These files should be further evaluated for suspected interferences prior to use for publication.
Extreme values were reviewed to evaluate possible interferences and disturbances caused by boat operations (intakes/motors resuspending bed sediment, filter changes, etc). If interferences or disturbance were suspected, the data were censored.
Field measurements were compared to laboratory measurements collected at the 33 sites to verify instrument performance. Intercomparisons among similar and redundant measurements were performed to screen data for bubbles and other interferences.
dataqual.complete:
Data set is considered complete for the information presented, as described in the abstract. Users are advised to read the rest of the metadata record carefully for additional details.
This dataset contains time periods with missing data that are either reported as blank cells or removed from the dataset and are indicated as large jumps in time in the DateTime value.
The authors have removed data that do not meet the reporting standards due to many reasons including but not limited to: clogged sample lines, instrument calibration or purging times, pumps turned off, clogged filters, unstable or unexpected sample readings, or other indications that the data does not meet quality standards for reporting.

horizpa.horizpar: All measurements initially processed with Python (v3.0) were plotted for review within one week of collection to verify the GPS accuracy and to verify that they were within expected and reasonable ranges. When GPS data did not follow the transect, data were pulled from a second, backup GPS unit deployed on the boat.

vertacc.vertaccr: No formal positional accuracy tests were conducted

procstep.procdesc:
WATER QUALITY MAPPING METHODS
The study is based on the assessment and comparison of four broad-area high-resolution water quality mapping surveys of the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta (SSJRD) conducted in October 2019, January 2020, July 2020, and April 2021. Each survey was comprised of three successive days of high-resolution mapping centered on a LANDSAT8 satellite overpass (Landsat 8 | U.S. Geological Survey (usgs.gov)). Data were collected at high speed to minimize to the extent to which tidal currents obscure water quality gradients. Underway water sampling and onboard analyses: Surveys were conducted using the USGS R/V Mary Landsteiner on successive days: October 9, 10, and 11, 2019; January 13, 14, and 15, 2020; July 7, 8 and 9, 2020; and R/V George R. Aiken on April 5, 6, and 7, 2021. On each day data were collected during daylight hours with mapping starting approximately two hours prior to the tidal stage conditions of the Landsat8 flyover scheduled for the second day of mapping and continuing for approximately four hours each day such that data collection occurred as close to the tidal conditions of the satellite image as possible for all three days.
For analysis, sample water was continuously pumped onto the boat through two flow-paths while underway at speeds up to 30 mph (13 m/s) using a pick-up tube mounted at a fixed depth of approximately 1 m below the surface, routed through a 179-micron screen to remove large debris and into a pressure-compensated manifold that maintained system pressure at a prescribed level irrespective of boat speed (Downing et al., 2016). A two-stage debubbler was used to remove bubbles that can interfere with optical measurements. Sample water was split and separated for each analytical flow path, with constant flow rates maintained by metering the discharge line, except for the flow path serving the open split interface, which was metered on the feed line. Flows were continuously monitored using a sight gauge for each flow path. Two manifolds were used to deliver water to various water quality instrumentation.
One manifold delivered water to the first flow-path (referred to as the FTS flow-path) similar to the 'Delta Water Quality Mapping 2020 Methods.pdf' listed on the O'Donnell and others, 2020 data release: (1) A flow-through system consisting of a thermosalinograph that recorded temperature and conductance (Sea-Bird Scientific SB45 (TSG), Belluve, WA); fluorometers that measured fluorescence of chlorophyll-a (fCHLA) and fluorescence of dissolved organic matter (fDOM, WETLabs WETstar (WS), Philomath, OR); a beam transmissometer that recorded transmittance and attenuation (WETLabs model C-Star transmissometer (CStar), Philomath, OR), a nitrate (NO3) analyzer (SUNA V2; Sea-Bird Scientific, Bellevue, WA). (2) A flow chamber for a multiparameter water quality sonde (YSI EXO2; Xylem Inc. (EXO), Rye Brook, NY) equipped with sensors to measure temperature, specific conductance (SpC), turbidity, pH, dissolved oxygen (DO), fDOM and fCHLA. (3) A fluorometer designed to measure different algal classes (FluoroProbe III (FP); BBE Moldaenke, Kiel, Germany).
The second flow-path (referred to as the ACS flow-path) pulled river water in the same manner as the FTS flow-path, but the water was further split into two paths, one that contained a 0.2 micron filter (Barney Corporation, INC., Hilliard, OH) and the other that did not contain a filter. Each flow-path was equipped with a spectrometer (ac-s Spectral Absorption and Attenuation Sensor | Sea-Bird Scientific, Bellevue, Washington | Sea-Bird (seabird.com)) capable of measuring attenuation and absorption spectra throughout the visible wavelength range. This set-up allowed for the simultaneous measurement of absorption (a) and attenuation (c) within the filter-passing (dissolved) fraction and the particulate fraction of the water. While underway, the analyzer was run in continuous mode. The ac-s spectrometer was connected to a stand-alone computer and collected data through its native software. All instrumentation was cleaned, and calibrations were checked prior to each use following the manufacturer's recommendation or as described below. Data for most instruments was recorded at 1-second frequency on a single data logger (CR6, Campbell Scientific, Logan, UT) together with a timestamp and boat position obtained from a high-resolution global positioning system receiver (16X-HVS, Garmin, Olathe, KS). The FluoroProbe logged data internally and to the host software. All data were displayed in real time, so scientists on board could respond when they observed changes relevant to study objectives, or if issues with flow or instrument performance were noted.
REFERENCES
Downing, B.D., Bergamaschi, B.A., Kendall, C., Kraus, T.E., Dennis, K.J., Carter, J.A. and Von Dessonneck, T.S., 2016. Using continuous underway isotope measurements to map water residence time in hydrodynamically complex tidal environments. Environmental science & technology, 50(24), pp.13387-13396. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.est.6b05745
O'Donnell, K ., Kraus, T.E.C., Downing, B.D., Hansen, A.M., Etheridge, A.B., Stumpner, E.B. , Richardson, E.T., Hansen, J.A., Soto Perez, J., Delascagigas, A., Sturgeon, C.L., Von Hoyningen Huene, B.L., Gelber, A D., Uebner, M.Q., Bergamaschi, B.A., 2021, Assessing spatial variability of nutrients, phytoplankton and related water-quality constituents in the California Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta at the landscape scale: 2020 High-resolution mapping surveys. U.S. Geological Survey, Data Release. https://doi.org/10.5066/P90VYUBX.

PROCESS FTS FLOW PATH DATA
All data in the FTS flow path were processed using Python (v3.0) code that removes suspect data associated with pumps; applies temperature, turbidity, inner-filtering effects (IFE), and instrument offset corrections; and smooths the data using a 20-sec median running value.

REVIEW AND TRIM FTS DATA
The processed FTS data file was reviewed and trimmed to account for any additional or residual influence of measurements collected during breaks in the FTS pumping, filter changes, and(or) disturbance events that were not removed during the processing step.

PROCESS ACS FLOW PATH DATA
Python (v3.0) code was written to process spectral data collected using a SeaBird spectrophotometer (Downing and others, 2009) and stored using acs host software program Compass. Each Seabird ac-s instrument has two channels: absorbance (a-) and attenuation (c-). Subscripts of processed data represent the non-filtered (pg) and filtered (g) flowpaths, generating four spectrophotometric measurements for a single sampling timestamp (ag, apg, cg, cpg). Those measurements can then be used to calculate particulate absorption (ap = apg - ag) and particulate attenuation (cp = cpg - cg) spectra.
Summary of data processing steps: 1) Read in and concatenate all acs data files into one dataframe and perform the following operations on each:
2) Convert acs data file timestamp from the cumulative ‘milliseconds since file started’ to the convention ‘mm/dd/yyyy hh:mm:ss’.
3) Reduce acs timestamp from 4 Hz to 1 Hz using a median.
4) Assign any lags/timeshift to acs timestamps. Lags are assigned based on the specific flow path (filtered (g) or nonfiltered (pg)).
5) Create columns for filter status (filtered or nonfiltered) and ac-s serial number (265 or 332).
6) Read in the following information from the FTS file: timestamp, temperature, salinity, latitude, longitude, and fDOM.
7) Read in wavelength specific temperature and salinity correction factors and reshape data frame.
8) Read in blank data files.
9) Cubic spline acs data. Wavelengths between the two instruments (SN 265 and 332) and their flow paths (filtered or nonfiltered) were not consistent. We used a cubic spline interpolation to generate spectra ranging from 400 to 716 nm every 4 nm. https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/reference/api/pandas.DataFrame.interpolate.html method = cubicspline
10) Separate a- and c-channel data into two separate dataframes.
11) Match hyperspectral dependency data with acs data.
12) Apply temperature and salinity corrections provided by Seabird to acs field data.
13) Apply temperature correction to blank data.
14) Blank correct acs field data.
15) Apply scatter correction to a-channel data. Reference wavelength is 716.
16) Apply 20 second running median.
17) Calculate ap (apg – ag), cp (cpg – cg).
REFERENCES:
Downing, B.D., Boss, E., Bergamaschi, B.A., Fleck, J.A., Lionberger, M.A., Ganju, N.K., Schoellhamer, D.H. and Fujii, R., 2009. Quantifying fluxes and characterizing compositional changes of dissolved organic matter in aquatic systems in situ using combined acoustic and optical measurements. Limnology and Oceanography: Methods, 7(1), pp.119-131.

REVIEW ACS FLOW PATH DATA
Measurements were evaluated by comparisons to similar measurements collected using other instruments and laboratory measurements from samples collected at 33 sites across the SSJRD. Data quality was further evaluated by verifying that filtered channel data were lower than respective non-filtered channels minimizing the number of negative values for the calculated particulate (ap, cp) values.

MERGE ACS MEASUREMENTS WITH GPS LOCATION DATA
Location data were added to each of the spectral data tables from the FTS_water_quality_20s_median.csv file using standard functions within Excel. Gaps in location data are due to missing GPS information for the times listed and are reported as blank cells in the dataset.

MERGE EACH MAPPING EVENT INTO SINGLE FILE
All data collected and processed by mapping event were merged by DATE:TIME into a single timeseries for FTS and ACS datasets.

overview.eaover:
This data release contains tabular data from water collected during four sampling events by boat through automatic flow through systems while the boat was in motion over the study area. The seven tables presented are in tabular, comma separated value (*.csv) format, as follows:
FTS_water_quality_20s_median.csv - optical properties and water quality parameters collected from Wetlabs (WLM), Seabird Submersible Ultraviolet Nitrate analyzer (SUNA), Seabird Model SB45 Thermosalinograph (TSG), YSI EXO2 (XOM), and Fluoroprobe BBE Instrument (FPM) field sensors
FTS_acs_ag.csv - spectral absorption data for filtered water, calculated value [apg - ag = ap], from data collected using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
FTS_acs_ap.csv - spectral absorption data for particulates from water using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
FTS_acs_apg.csv - spectral absorption data for nonfiltered water using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
FTS_acs_cg.csv - spectral attenuation data for filtered water using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
FTS_acs_cp.csv - spectral attenuation data for water particulate, calculated value [cpg-cg = cp], from data collected using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
FTS_acs_cpg.csv - spectral attenuation data for nonfiltered water collected using a Seabird Spectral Absorption and Attenuation field sensor (field ACS monitor).
overview.eadetcit: All entity and attributes for this data release are producer defined and fully described within this document. No outside data dictionaries have been used. All tabular data presented are from field measurements that have been cleaned for data that does not meet the reporting standards.

cntaddr.address: Denver Federal Center, Building 810, Mail Stop 302

distinfo.distliab: Unless otherwise stated, all data, metadata and related materials are considered to satisfy the quality standards relative to the purpose for which the data were collected. Although these data and associated metadata have been reviewed for accuracy and completeness and approved for release by the U.S. Geological Survey (USGS), no warranty expressed or implied is made regarding the display or utility of the data on any other system or for general or scientific purposes, nor shall the act of distribution constitute any such warranty.
